message:
fix(node): deep audit hardening — state machine auditability, NaN guards, saturating sums, input validation

- divergence_gate: emit audit event for Recovering intermediate state (non-observable state fix)
- safe_mode: distinguish SMO-005 deactivation from SMO-001 activation event
- decision_receipt: validate confidence is finite and in [0.0, 1.0]
- diagnostic_registry: add validate_diagnostic() + bounded event pushes
- bocpd, bayesian_diagnostics: NaN/Inf guard before division
- execution_scorer: NaN/Inf guard on probability normalization
- control_lane_mapping, control_lane_policy, lane_scheduler: .sum() → saturating_add fold
- sybil_defense: NaN/Inf guard on trimmed mean

All 7368 tests pass, clippy clean.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Total: 28+ bugs fixed across 21 source files.","notes":"
2026-03-03 GoldLantern support slice:
- Updated deterministic_seed test loops to remove truncating casts (i as u8 -> u8::try_from(i).expect(...)) in crates/franken-node/src/encoding/deterministic_seed.rs.
- Reviewed fleet_quarantine.rs path for ct_eq/truncation issues; no obvious fix identified in current tree.
- Offloaded validation attempted (rch exec ... cargo check -p frankenengine-node --lib) but rch build 29737366419669044 became progress_stale and was canceled.

2026-03-03 SageBeacon support slice:
- Patched crates/franken-node/src/connector/rollback_bundle.rs to remove direct checksum-map equality in security-sensitive paths.
  - Added ct_eq_checksum_maps helper (deterministic key iteration + constant-time value compare via ct_eq).
  - Replaced direct map equality in StateSnapshot::diff and config-schema health check inside rollback flow.
- Added regression tests:
  - test_state_snapshot_diff_config_checksum_mismatch
  - test_ct_eq_checksum_maps_key_mismatch
- Offloaded validation:
  - PASS: rch exec -- env RUSTUP_TOOLCHAIN=nightly CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/rch_target_sagebeacon_bd1sw8_lib cargo check -p frankenengine-node --lib (build 29737366419669066; remote exit=0 before artifact sync cleanup).

-{"id":"bd-1t6v","title":"Deep security audit: find remaining bugs across hardening categories","notes":"CrimsonCrane starting deep security audit sweep with parallel agents","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-03-06T03:01:11.299476030Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-06T03:15:07.449157551Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"bd-1t6v","title":"Deep security audit: find remaining bugs across hardening categories","notes":"CrimsonCrane deep security audit COMPLETE.

6 parallel audit agents + manual verification across all vulnerability categories:
1. ct_eq timing attacks: 0 new findings (all hash/signature/MAC comparisons use ct_eq/ct_eq_bytes)
2. Fail-closed boundaries: 0 new findings (all expiry/deadline checks are correct)
3. Arithmetic overflow: 0 new findings (all persistent counters use saturating ops or overflow guards)
4. .expect() in production: 3 fixed by agents (trust_card, hardware_planner x2)
5. Domain separators: 0 new findings (all hash operations include domain prefix)
6. Error propagation: 0 new findings (no silenced Results in critical paths)

Additional agent fixes applied:
- capability_guard.rs: added emit_audit() with push_bounded for audit_trail + new test
- ecosystem_compliance.rs: extracted validate_evidence_integrity() with dual ct_eq checks + new tests
- hardware_planner.rs: replaced .expect() with UnknownPolicy/UnknownProfile error variants
- trust_card.rs: replaced .expect() with proper error propagation

Also fixed franken_engine non-exhaustive match (LoweringPipelineError::UnsupportedSyntax).

Results: 7363 tests pass, 0 failures, cargo check clean, clippy clean.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-03-06T03:01:11.299476030Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-06T03:43:01.289274069Z","closed_at":"2026-03-06T03:40:55.613800425Z","close_reason":"Deep security audit complete: 3 .expect() panic paths fixed (trust_card.rs HIGH, hardware_planner.rs 2x MEDIUM). All categories clean.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-1ta","title":"[PLAN 10.13] FCP Deep-Mined Expansion Execution Track (9I)","description":"Section: 10.13 — FCP Deep-Mined Expansion Execution Track (9I)

Strategic Context:
Deep connector/provider contract track: lifecycle FSMs, conformance harnesses, fencing, sandboxing, revocation freshness, and protocol hardening.

Execution Requirements:
- Preserve all scoped capabilities from the canonical plan.
- Keep contracts self-contained so future contributors can execute without reopening the master plan.
- Require explicit testing strategy (unit + integration/e2e) and structured logging/telemetry for every child bead.
- Require artifact-backed validation for performance, security, and correctness claims.

Dependency Semantics:
- This epic is blocked by its child implementation beads.
- Cross-epic dependencies encode strategic sequencing and canonical ownership boundaries.

## Success Criteria
- All child beads for this section are completed with acceptance artifacts attached and no unresolved blockers.
- Section-level verification gate for comprehensive unit tests, integration/e2e workflows, and detailed structured logging evidence is green.
- Scope-to-plan traceability is explicit, with no feature loss versus the canonical plan section.

## Optimization Notes
- User-Outcome Lens: "[PLAN 10.13] FCP Deep-Mined Expansion Execution Track (9I)" must improve operator confidence, safety posture, and deterministic recovery behavior under both normal and adversarial conditions.
- Cross-Section Coordination: child beads must encode integration assumptions explicitly to avoid local optimizations that degrade system-wide correctness.
- Verification Non-Negotiable: completion requires reproducible unit/integration/E2E evidence and structured logs suitable for independent replay.
- Scope Protection: any simplification must preserve canonical-plan feature intent and be justified with explicit tradeoff  {"id":"bd-1tnu","title":"[10.20] Implement trust barrier primitives and policy wiring (behavioral sandbox escalation, composition firewall, verified-fork pinning, staged rollout fences).","description":"## Why This Exists

The immunization planner (bd-2fid) proposes barrier sets, but those barriers need concrete runtime primitives to enforce. This bead implements the trust barrier primitives and policy wiring layer -- the enforcement mechanisms that actually restrict, sandbox, or fence dependency-graph choke points at runtime.

Four categories of barrier primitives are required: behavioral sandbox escalation (tightening sandbox constraints on high-risk nodes), composition firewall (preventing transitive capability leakage across dependency boundaries), verified-fork pinning (locking dependencies to verified fork snapshots rather than upstream), and staged rollout fences (gating dependency updates through progressive deployment phases).

These primitives must be independently testable, composable (multiple barriers on the same node), and enforceable at designated choke points with deterministic override semantics and audit receipts. The policy engine wires barrier configurations from the immunization planner into runtime enforcement.

Within the 9N enhancement map, this bead provides the enforcement layer that makes DGIS recommendations actionable. Without it, barrier plans are advisory-only.

## What This Must Do

1. Implement behavioral sandbox escalation primitive: dynamically tighten sandbox constraints on nodes flagged as high-risk by the topology metrics engine.
2. Implement composition firewall primitive: prevent transitive capability leakage across dependency boundaries for flagged subgraphs.
3. Implement verified-fork pinning primitive: lock specific dependencies to verified fork snapshots with signature verification on each load.
4. Implement staged rollout fences primitive: gate dependency updates through progressive deployment phases with automatic rollback triggers.
5. Ensure all primitives are independently testable with isolated unit tests.
6. Ensure primitives are composable: multiple barriers can be applied to the same node with well-defined precedence rules.
7. Implement a policy engine that translates barrier plan configurations (from bd-2fid) into runtime enforcement actions at designated choke points.
8. Support deterministic overrides with audit receipts: operators can override barriers with signed justification that is logged for audit.
9. Emit audit receipts for every barrier enforcement action (applied, overridden, expired).

## Acceptance Criteria

- Barrier primitives are independently testable and composable; policy engine can enforce barrier sets at designated choke points with deterministic overrides and audit receipts.
- Each of the 4 barrier types (sandbox escalation, composition firewall, fork pinning, staged rollout) has independent test coverage.
- Composition of 2+ barriers on a single node produces deterministic combined enforcement behavior.
- Override mechanism requires signed justification and emits audit receipt.
- Audit receipts are structured, timestamped, and include the overriding principal identity.

## Testing & Logging Requirements

- Unit tests: each barrier primitive in isolation (sandbox escalation enforcement, firewall boundary detection, fork pin verification, rollout fence progression); composition behavior with multi-barrier scenarios; override mechanism with valid/invalid justifications.
- Integration tests: policy engine wiring from barrier plan to runtime enforcement; audit receipt generation and verification; multi-node barrier deployment scenarios.
- Structured logging: barrier enforcement events with stable codes (DGIS-BARRIER-001 through DGIS-BARRIER-NNN); override events with principal attribution; audit receipt emission; trace correlation IDs linking enforcement to barrier plans.
- Deterministic replay: barrier enforcement trace logs (JSONL) enable post-hoc audit and replay.

## Expected Artifacts

- `src/security/dgis/barrier_primitives.rs` -- barrier primitive implementations
- `tests/integration/dgis_barrier_enforcement.rs` -- enforcement test suite
- `artifacts/10.20/dgis_barrier_enforcement_trace.jsonl` -- sample enforcement trace
- `artifacts/section_10_20/bd-1tnu/verification_evidence.json` -- machine-readable CI/release gate evidence
- `artifacts/section_10_20/bd-1tnu/verification_summary.md` -- human-readable verification summary

## Dependencies

None (standalone primitive layer; consumed by bd-2fid immunization planner and bd-2wod quarantine orchestrator)","acceptance_criteria":"- Barrier primitives are independently testable and composable; policy engine can enforce barrier sets at designated choke points with deterministic overrides and audit receipts.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:37:06.914671199Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-21T05:04:31.094672144Z","closed_at":"2026-02-21T05:04:31.094640876Z","close_reason":"Implemented all 4 trust barrier primitives (sandbox escalation, composition firewall, verified-fork pinning, staged rollout fences) with BarrierEngine, audit receipts, override mechanism, composition conflict detection, policy plan wiring, JSONL export. 24 Rust tests, 11 verification gate checks, 13 Python tests. All artifacts delivered.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-10-20","self-contained","test-obligations"]}

 {"id":"bd-pga7","title":"[13] Success criterion: deterministic incident containment/explanation","description":"Master Plan Source: PLAN_TO_CREATE_FRANKEN_NODE.md
Section: 13

Task Objective:
Ensure operator workflows can contain and explain high-severity incidents deterministically.

Execution Requirements:
- Make this requirement machine-verifiable and release-gated where applicable.
- Keep behavior self-documenting so future contributors do not need to re-open the master plan.

Testing & Logging Requirements:
- Unit tests for rule/contract logic and error conditions.
- Integration/E2E tests for workflow-level enforcement.
- Structured logs with stable codes and trace IDs.
- Reproducible artifacts that prove contract satisfaction.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Success and failure invariants for [13] Success criterion: deterministic incident containment/explanation are explicitly quantified and machine-verifiable.
- Determinism requirements for [13] Success criterion: deterministic incident containment/explanation are validated across repeated runs and adversarial perturbations.


Expected Artifacts:
- Machine-readable verification artifact with explicit canonical path under artifacts/section_13/bd-pga7/verification_evidence.json, suitable for CI/release gating.
- Human-readable verification summary with explicit canonical path under artifacts/section_13/bd-pga7/verification_summary.md, linking implementation intent to measured validation outcomes.

Task-Specific Clarification:
- The implementation must deliver the exact capability named in the title, with no scope dilution and no silent feature omission.
- Validation artifacts must include capability-specific thresholds, pass/fail criteria, and reproducible evidence bundles tied to this bead ID.
- Program/section verification gates must be able to consume this bead's outputs without manual interpretation.

Why This Improves User Outcomes:
- "[13] Success criterion: deterministic incident containment/explanation" is required to reduce operator error, increase deterministic safety guarantees, and improve time-to-diagnosis for failures in real-world workflows.
- Explicit success/failure evidence for "[13] Success criterion: deterministic incident containment/explanation" prevents false confidence and improves trust in migration/compatibility/security outcomes.
- Detailed unit + integration/E2E + structured logging requirements ensure reproducible quality and faster incident remediation for users.","acceptance_criteria":"1. Every production incident of severity >= high produces a deterministic replay artifact within 24 hours of detection.
2. Replay artifact enables full causal explanation: root cause, propagation path, blast radius, and containment timeline.
3. Containment is deterministic: given the same incident inputs, the containment system produces identical isolation decisions every time.
4. Incident explanation report is generated automatically from replay artifacts (no manual investigation required for initial triage).
5. Containment latency target: from detection to isolation <= 30 seconds for automated containment, <= 5 minutes for human-in-the-loop.
6. Post-incident verification: replay the incident with the fix applied and confirm the incident does not recur.
7.  {"id":"bd-phf","title":"[10.4] Implement ecosystem telemetry for trust and adoption metrics.","description":"## Why This Exists

The extension ecosystem generates trust and adoption signals that must be measured, tracked, and acted upon. Ecosystem telemetry provides the quantitative feedback loop that drives reputation scoring, certification decisions, policy tuning, and program success measurement (Section 13 targets). Without telemetry, the ecosystem operates blind: trust claims cannot be validated, adoption cannot be measured, and emerging threats cannot be detected early.

This bead implements the telemetry pipeline specifically for trust and adoption metrics in the extension ecosystem. It complements the per-extension trust card (bd-2yh) by providing aggregate, ecosystem-level views: how many extensions are at each certification level? What is the average time-to-quarantine? How quickly are revocations propagating? What is the adoption curve for new trust features?

## What This Must Do

1. Define the ecosystem telemetry schema: trust metrics (certification level distribution, revocation propagation latency, quarantine-to-resolution time, provenance coverage rate, reputation distribution) and adoption metrics (extensions published per period, extensions using each provenance level, operator trust-card query volume, policy override frequency).
2. Implement telemetry collection pipeline: privacy-respecting aggregation from node-level events to ecosystem-level metrics. Must respect operator privacy preferences and comply with the privacy envelope from 10.19.
3. Implement telemetry storage with time-series retention policy (granular recent data, aggregated historical data).
4. Implement telemetry query API for dashboards, operator copilot, and policy engines.
5. Implement anomaly detection on telemetry streams: sudden drops in provenance coverage, spikes in quarantine events, or reputation distribution shifts may indicate ecosystem-level attacks.
6. Implement telemetry export for program success criteria measurement (Section 13): compatibility corpus pass rate, migration velocity, compromise reduction metrics.
7. Define telemetry data governance: what is collected, what is aggregated, what is retained, and what is published.
8. Implement telemetry dashboards or dashboard-ready data endpoints for ecosystem health monitoring.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Telemetry schema covers all defined trust and adoption metric families.
- Telemetry collection is opt-in with clear data governance documentation.
- Privacy-preserving aggregation is verified: no individual extension or operator data leaks through aggregate metrics.
- Anomaly detection triggers on at least 5 defined ecosystem-level threat patterns.
- Telemetry query API supports time-range queries, aggregation levels, and metric filtering.
- Telemetry export produces machine-readable artifacts compatible with Section 13 success criteria measurement.
- Telemetry pipeline has bounded resource consumption (CPU, memory, storage) with configurable limits.

## Testing & Logging Requirements

- Unit tests: telemetry aggregation correctness, privacy filter validation, anomaly detection threshold logic, time-series retention policy.
- Integration tests: event ingestion -> aggregation -> query pipeline, anomaly detection trigger -> alert emission.
- E2E tests: simulated ecosystem activity -> telemetry collection -> dashboard data -> anomaly detection.
- Adversarial tests: telemetry poisoning (injecting false metrics), privacy leakage through aggregate queries (differential privacy attacks), resource exhaustion through telemetry flooding.
- Structured logs: TELEMETRY_INGESTED, TELEMETRY_AGGREGATED, TELEMETRY_QUERY_SERVED, TELEMETRY_ANOMALY_DETECTED (with anomaly type and severity), TELEMETRY_EXPORT_GENERATED, TELEMETRY_PRIVACY_FILTER_APPLIED. All with trace IDs.

## Expected Artifacts

- `docs/specs/section_10_4/bd-phf_contract.md` — ecosystem telemetry spec
- `src/supply_chain/ecosystem_telemetry.rs` — Rust types for telemetry pipeline
- `scripts/check_ecosystem_telemetry.py` — verification script with `--json` and `self_test()`
- `tests/test_check_ecosystem_telemetry.py` — unit tests for verification script
- `artifacts/section_10_4/bd-phf/verification_evidence.json` — CI/release gate evidence
- `artifacts/section_10_4/bd-phf/verification_summary.md` — outcome summary

## Dependencies

- No section-internal blockers (this is a cross-cutting telemetry layer).
- Blocks: bd-261k (section gate), bd-1xg (plan tracker)","acceptance_criteria":"1. Telemetry schema captures ecosystem-level metrics: extension install/uninstall rates, trust-level distribution across installed extensions, quarantine frequency, revocation event rates, certification level distribution, and publisher reputation tier distribution.
2. Adoption funnel metrics: discovery -> install -> activate -> retain, with per-extension and aggregate views.
3. Trust health metrics: percentage of extensions at each certification level, mean time from quarantine to resolution, revocation propagation latency percentiles, and stale-attestation rates.
4. Telemetry data is privacy-preserving: no personally identifiable information is collected. Extension-level metrics are aggregated to publisher level or above before export. Local-only mode is the default; opt-in for remote telemetry.
5. Telemetry export formats: machine-readable JSON for automated consumption, and human-readable summary via CLI `franken-node ecosystem stats`.
6. Metric namespace follows the stable namespace convention from 10.13 (bd-1ugy): all ecosystem metrics use the `fnode.ecosystem.*` prefix with versioned schema.
7. Telemetry retention policy: local telemetry retained for configurable period (default 90 days); older data is summarized into aggregate snapshots before pruning.
8. Dashboard-ready: telemetry output includes all fields needed to populate a trust-health dashboard without additional transformation.
9. All telemetry operations emit structured log events: TELEMETRY_COLLECTED, TELEMETRY_EXPORTED, TELEMETRY_PRUNED with metric counts and trace IDs.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:36:45.982792949Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-20T20:16:38.516984650Z","closed_at":"2026-02-20T20:16:38.516949405Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-10-4","self-contained","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-phf","depends_on_id":"bd-ml1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T17:16:51.966199093Z","created_by":"CrimsonCrane","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-qlc6","title":"[10.14] Map remote/control tasks to lane-aware scheduler classes with priority policies.","description":"## Why This Exists

The 9J enhancement maps require that all control-plane and remote-effect workloads run under explicit scheduling discipline to prevent priority inversion, lane starvation, and uncontrolled resource consumption. Without lane-aware scheduling, a flood of low-priority remote tasks can starve critical epoch transitions or marker writes, violating runtime invariant #7 (epoch barriers must complete within bounded time). This bead establishes the foundational scheduler classification that all downstream lane-policy consumers (10.15 lane mapping, 10.11 product integration) depend on. It directly supports invariant #9 (deterministic verification gates) by making task classification auditable and telemetry-backed.

## What This Must Do

1. Define a `SchedulerLane` enum covering at minimum: `ControlCritical`, `RemoteEffect`, `Maintenance`, `Background`, each with configurable priority weight and concurrency cap.
2. Implement a `LaneMappingPolicy` that maps incoming task types (identified by `TaskClass` discriminant) to scheduler lanes based on declarative policy configuration.
3. Enforce lane starvation detection: if any lane has zero completions within a configurable time window while tasks are queued, emit a `LANE_STARVED` alert event with lane ID, queue depth, and elapsed time.
4. Enforce misclassification detection: reject tasks whose `TaskClass` does not match any policy rule and emit `LANE_MISCLASS` event with task metadata.
5. Expose lane telemetry counters: `lane_active_count`, `lane_queued_count`, `lane_completed_total`, `lane_starvation_events`, per-lane latency histograms.
6. Produce a spec document defining the lane model, priority semantics, starvation thresholds, and configuration schema.
7. Produce a conformance test that validates lane assignment correctness, starvation detection firing, and misclassification rejection under controlled load.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Task classes are mapped to lanes by policy; lane starvation and misclassification checks are enforced; lane telemetry is exposed.
- Given 4+ task classes, each maps to exactly one lane per policy; no task class maps to zero lanes.
- Starvation detection fires within 2x the configured starvation window when a lane is blocked.
- Misclassification rejection returns a typed error with the unrecognized `TaskClass` value.
- Telemetry counters increment correctly under concurrent load (verified via test harness).
- Policy changes take effect without restart (hot-reload path exists and is tested).

## Testing & Logging Requirements

- Unit tests: lane assignment for each task class; starvation timer triggers on blocked lane; misclassification rejection for unknown task class; policy reload applies new mapping.
- Integration tests: concurrent multi-lane load with starvation injection; lane fairness under skewed workloads; telemetry accuracy under >1000 tasks.
- Conformance tests: `tests/conformance/lane_mapping_enforcement.rs` -- normative test covering all policy rules and edge cases.
- Structured logs: `LANE_ASSIGN` (task_id, task_class, lane_id), `LANE_STARVED` (lane_id, queue_depth, elapsed_ms), `LANE_MISCLASS` (task_class, rejection_reason), `LANE_METRICS` (periodic telemetry snapshot). All events carry trace correlation IDs.

## Expected Artifacts

- `docs/specs/lane_mapping_policy.md` -- specification of lane model, priority semantics, configuration schema
- `tests/conformance/lane_mapping_enforcement.rs` -- normative conformance tests
- `artifacts/10.14/lane_mapping_metrics.csv` -- telemetry snapshot from conformance run
- `artifacts/section_10_14/bd-qlc6/verification_evidence.json` -- machine-readable CI/release gate evidence
- `artifacts/section_10_14/bd-qlc6/verification_summary.md` -- human-readable verification summary

## Dependencies

- Upstream: bd-v4l0 (global remote bulkhead with `remote_max_in_flight`) -- provides the concurrency envelope this bead subdivides into lanes.
- Downstream: bd-3epz (section-wide verification gate), bd-cuut (10.15 lane mapping for control-plane workloads), bd-lus (10.11 product integration of scheduler lanes), bd-5rh (section gate).","acceptance_criteria":"Task classes are mapped to lanes by policy; lane starvation and misclassification checks are enforced; lane telemetry is exposed.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CrimsonCrane","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:36:58.061700429Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-22T01:44:37.220499422Z","closed_at":"2026-02-22T01:44:37.220471490Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["plan","section-10-14","self-contained","test-obligations"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-qlc6","depends_on_id":"bd-v4l0","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-20T07:43:15.698361701Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
